headlines.title: CITY SCHOOLS GAIN 657; COUNTY 284
headlines.subtitle: Next Year's Increase Expected to Be Greater Because of Age Limit Change. FINAL TABLE COMPLETED Districts 3 and 14 in Fort Worth Only Ones to Show Decreases.
headlines.fullText: There are 613 more children of school age. 7 to 11, in Fort Worth than in 1912, according to the final figures of the scholastic census. Last year's figures were 12.574. The 1913 figures are 13,183, White children Increased 657. Negro children decreased. The total mcrease in the county is 284. The largest increase in city districts is in District No. 1, which shows 129 more this year than 1912. District No. 10 is next with an increase of 128. With the exception of Districts Nos. 3 and 14, all others showed slight increases. Total Pupils 22,306. The total number of children of school age in the county and city is 22.306. These figures will be greatly increased next year as the last legislature raised the school age from 7 to 17. The following tables show comparative figures for city and county schools:

headlines.title: Colorado Tour Voting Draws Crowds to Balloting Places
headlines.subtitle: Many Are Saving Up Votes. Clubs of Five Are Having Greatest Success.
headlines.fullText: Just how big a success The Star-Telegram's Free Vacation Trip to Colorado Contest will score was Indicated Wednesday when hundreds of persons visited the bureaus exchanging purchase slips for ballots, Wednesday was the first exchange day. Covey & Martin's drug store and Rienfro's Ninth street pharmacy were filled throughout the day with scores of candidates and their hundreds of friends busily engaged in exchanging sales slips they had collected, for ballots. A wrong impression regarding the casting of the ballots appears to have been created among some of the candidates. They believe the ballots must be cast the same day they are obtained. This is not true. The purchase slips must be exchanged for ballots on Wednesdays, but the ballots thus obtained can be voted at any time during the campaign. Candidates should lose no time in impressing upon their workers and friends the necessity of making their exchanges and getting the ballots in exchange for the purchase slips. but voting the ballots whenever they choose. So many of the candidates are holding back their votes that the standing of the candidates will not be published again during the campaign. After the campaign closes the total vote of each candidate will be published, but no other statement of their standing will be made. The list published today does not indicate the vote of the candidates. It does not show the vote of the campaign. The names will be printed merely for the purpose of affording the reading public an opportunity of seeing their names and voting for them. May Vote for Five. The most successful candidates so far are those who are organizing clubs of five; and all candidates not working along this line are urged to do so. There are five to win. They should impress upon their friends that they can vote for five as easily as for one. As an Mustration, Wednesday, the next exchange day, thirty votes will be given for each 1-cent purchase, or 300 with each $1 purchase. This means that you can cast for yourself and four other candidates, giving each the full 300 votes. Between now and next Wednesday candidates should exert themselves to see that their friends get downtown and make purchases. In this connection there are many amusing incidents coming to light. One woman on the south side borrowed flour from a neighbor Tuesday. She explained that she would wait until Wednesday to buy so she could buy and get votes to be cast for some candidate she was interested in.

headlines.title: ROGERS DENIES HE MENTIONED MONEY IN BRATTON TALK
headlines.subtitle: (CONTINUED FROM PAGE ONE.)
headlines.fullText: East Fourth street bridge contract, but promised to help his friends on the other contract. "Harwood was my friend and I was glad to speak a good word for his friends," said Rogers. "I saw Judge Bratton twice that day. Once outside the county court clerk's office hall I stopped him and told him that I would like to see his friends get the contracts. I don't know whether I said this within the hearing of whoever I had been talking with. I forget who it was. "Later, downstairs, near the cigar stand, I again told Judge Bratton about my friends and I added, 'There will be money in it for me if they get the contracts.' Never Intended Pay. "I never mentioned paying him money and did not intend to. I merely asked him as a friend of mine to help these contractors in order that I might get some money to help pay my judgment to the county. I argued that the Tarrant Construction Company was a home concern and had done good work for the county and that it was entitled to the contract. "Jeff Johnson had come to me three or four times and reminded me that I had helped them get the West Seventh street bridge contract, for which I was not paid. He told me that if they got the East Fourth street contract, he would pay me for it. He did not say how much. I had no idea how much I was to get if the Tarrant Construction Company was successful. I never expect to collect from that company now. They were awarded the contract, but it was later rescinded. No Money Promised. "Brown Harwood being a friend of mine and Judge Bratton being a friend of his, in addition, I was certain that if I helped his friends, Ray McDonald & Co. get the Samuels avenue contract, he would have them pay me for my services, although nothing had been said about it. Other bidders had representatives before the county commissioners' court to present their claims and I thought it was my duty to do the same for my friends. Commissioner Rodgers stated to Judge Bratton that there would be something in it for him if his friends got the contracts. I had heard that Commissioners Estill and Reeves favored the Tarrant Construction Company. I did not speak to them about it because I was not so friendly with them and besides I felt sure that they would vote for the Tarrant Construction Company. On cross-examination, Rogers testified that he was in the livery stable business from the time he left the county clerk's office in 1908 until February, 1911, and that since that time he had an office first in the First National Bank building and at present in the Western National Bank building. He said he had not made a real estate transaction since he left office. "Isn't it a fact," Assistant County Attorney Wilson asked, "that your only business has been hanging around the commissioners' court up to the time of these exposures?" "No," he answered. "I have been about the courthouse a great deal attending to the county suit against me and I have talked with the commissioners about having the interest on that judgment remitted after the judgment was paid, didn't you?" "Not especially. I felt that I had been mistreated by the county and I retain his animus toward the county. He said he was not allowed even expenses for clerks and that he had to pay back to the county more than he made. The county commissioners told him, he said, that they knew it was morally wrong for him to have to pay back but that it was technically right. Rogers testified, on cross-examination, that he had talked with Bratton about good friends who wanted contracts and named the Tarrant Construction Company for the East Fourth street bridge and Ray McDonald & Co. for the Samuels avenue contract. "He said there would be 'a handful' in it for us," the witness said. "I don't know which." Denies "For Us" Clause. Rogers didn't say there would be "a handful or a five in it for us." Bratton denied that Rogers used the words "for us." Portions of his grand jury statements were introduced in evidence for purposes of impeaching him. Repudiates "Statements." In one statement Bratton is quoted as saying that Rogers told him there would be money in it for us," in another "for you" and in a third "a good piece of money for both of us." He repudiated all these statements. Bratton said he was before the grand jury four times. One time, he said, he was summoned by telephone at night and that at the end of a rigid grilling he was made to sign one of the three statements. He said that Rogers never used the word "us" but said that he "would like to see his friends get the contracts." Tells of Votes. The witness said he did vote for the Tarrant Construction Company but not for Ray McDonald & Co. He said that when Rogers told him there would be "a handful" or "a five" in it if his friends got the contracts, he answered "All right, if they are not too high." He said he told Rogers he liked the Tarrant Construction Company because the work it already had done for the county was satisfactory. On cross-examination the witness swore Rogers never offered him any sum of money to vote on any proposition. This was in refutation of the allegation in the indictment. When Bratton left the stand, Attorney William P. McLean Jr. asked for peremptory instructions to acquit on any of its allegations. Judge Buck overruled the motion. Bratton was the state's last witness.

headlines.title: Cupid Staggered but Not Beaten by Nationality
headlines.fullText: After obtaining a license to wed Miss Eugenia Mickay, Twenty-sixth and Clinton streets, Roman Linkiewig, 2515 North Houston street, learned that she was a Russian and Wednesday returned the license to County Clerk Beavers. Linkiewig is a Pole. Thursday morning Linkiewig appeared at the courthouse and requested that he be given the license again. After talking it over Wednesday night, he told Deputy Clerk Austin, he decided that it didn't matter about the nationality. Previous to this time he did not know she was a Russian. The license was returned to him.

headlines.title: City May Stock Lake Minnetonka With Game Fish
headlines.subtitle: Receives Offer of an Addition
headlines.fullText: Lake Minnetonka, the new city reservoir, already is becoming a haven for anglers. The damming of the West Fork has resulted in a large number of catfish being caught and as the water deepens the fishermen are having better luck. The line is encouraged as the dam is proposed upon completion of the dam to have it stocked with game fish. A screen will be placed on the big dam and it is hoped to keep the catfish and alligator gars out of the dam. Under the new state law the county commission has the power to stock such a body of water as Lake Minnetonka with good fish, and a movement along this line will be started when the dam is completed and the catfish are gotten out of the stream. Many good catches of catfish are being made daily.

headlines.title: Zoo, Voted Out, First Important Payments on Road Work Will Be Made.
headlines.fullText: Fort Worth's zoo, instead of being abolished, is being given additions. Without rescinding the action abolishing the zoo, the park board at its last meeting instructed the superintendent to take no action for the present in regard to disposing of the animals. Now the question will be brought to the front again by the offer of another deer to the zoo. The deer comes from Fowlerton, Texas.

headlines.title: COUNTY'S EXPENSES FOR PAST MONTH HIGHER
headlines.fullText: Tarrant county's expenses for June, which will be paid July 15, were nearly $100,000 higher than those for the previous month. Large payments on bridges now under construction boost the total. The first real payments on the $1,000,000 system of roads will be made this month. The total amount of contracts let is $1,053,039. Fifteen payments aggregating $313,518.58 have been made, leaving $739,520.42 to be paid to the Texas Building Company, the contractors. The following amounts are estimates of work done on the different roads: Fort Worth and Grapevine road, section A, $455.25; section B, $1,456.12. Dallas road, $5,907.81. Mansfield, section A, $1,188.11. Weatherford road, $4,072.44. Azle road, $4,341.59. Keller road, $3,950. Payments to contractors of the new viaducts are: West Seventh street, $35,911.29; East Fourth street, $34,166.61; Samuels avenue, $9,503.61; Main street, $30,642.85. These payments with the regular expenses and pay roll will amount to about $100,000.

headlines.title: Mail Carrier Who Dabbled in Stocks Dies a Suicide
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. ST. LOUIS, July 10.—H. W. Michel, a former mail carrier, who attempted suicide in the Merchants' Exchange Tuesday because he had lost $7,000 or $8,000 in speculation, died today.

headlines.title: Admits Four Thefts.
headlines.fullText: John Winfield, negro, plead guilty to four charges of theft. He was fined and sentenced in each, the fines totalling $70 and the sentences ninety days in jail. Winfield stole bicycles from four different persons.

headlines.title: BOY SCOUTS ENLISTED IN AMENDMENT FIGHT
headlines.fullText: R. M. Colquitt, manager of the constitutional amendment committee, has written a letter to the Fort Worth troop, Boy Scouts, asking their aid in favor of the adoption of an amendment which will be voted upon July 19. Mr. Colquitt says in part: "One of the primary objects of the Boy Scout movement is to get the boys interested in the educational and civic life of the country. It has been suggested that I ask you to explain to the boys the benefits that will be derived by the adoption of the amendment to section 18 of the constitution." Another letter written to Joseph A. Vera, scout master, asks that the literature which is sent him be distributed by the boys. Scouts throughout the state have taken up this work.

headlines.title: Warrant Out for Former Mayor on Speeding Charge
headlines.fullText: Warrants for the arrest of former Mayor W. D. Davis were issued Thursday morning by City Prosecutor Polk on complaints charging him with speeding and running his machine without lights. The complaints, filed in the corporation court preceding the issuance of the warrant, were made by Patrolman Coffey. The alleged offenses, it is said, occurred Wednesday night on Commerce street. It is understood that former Mayor Davis was not in the car at the time. The warrants had not been served up to Thursday afternoon.

headlines.title: COMMISSIONERS GUESTS AT GOOD ROADS MEETING
headlines.fullText: H. E. Duringer, W. C. Weeks, H. R. Wall and W. O. Reves, county commissioners; J. C. Travilla, engineer in charge of Tarrant county's $1,000,000 road system, and W. H. Beck, assistant secretary of the Chamber of Commerce, left Wednesday night for Corpus Christi to attend the annual meeting of the Texas Good Roads association. The commissioners go as guests of the Chamber of Commerce and that body will pay their expenses there and back. The party will return Sunday.
